exemplar.title: How does the duration that a person takes to slide on a copper plate is based on the thickness of a magnet.

detailedExemplarData.title: How does the duration that a person takes to slide on a copper plate is based on the thickness of a magnet.

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clearly identifies and motivates the topic with a relevant anecdote linking to rail safety.
Communicates the aim and research question sufficiently to guide the investigation.
Provides a mostly complete methodology with appropriate range of sources and procedural steps.
weaknesses: Research question phrasing is imprecise and grammatically weak, reducing focus.
Procedural descriptions are verbose and lack clear subâstep structure.
Some repetition and vagueness in explaining purpose and procedure undermines clarity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work meets the mid-band descriptors: topic and purpose are communicated adequately and the research question is clear but partially imprecise. Methodology is mostly complete with evidence of informed selection. However, wording flaws and procedural vagueness prevent a higher score in the 5-6 range. A score of 4 reflects this solid mid-band performance.

strengths: Background references to Faradayâs law, eddy currents and Newtonâs laws show relevant subject knowledge.
Correct use of the formula F = BILâsinâÎ¸ demonstrates understanding of electromagnetic forces.
Accurate categorization of magnet types indicates a good foundational grasp of magnetic materials.
weaknesses: Equations contain errors (e.g. misuse of Î¸ for flux) and inconsistent variable notation.
Key terms are often misspelled or misapplied (âFardayâs lawâ, âelectromagnetic induction forceâ).
Conceptual explanations are superficial and occasionally drift into irrelevant topics.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Knowledge application is competent, with mostly relevant source use and clear understanding of core concepts (band 3â4). However, inconsistent terminology, spelling mistakes and equation errors limit the work to the lower half of the mid-band, warranting a score of 3 out of 6.

strengths: Presents raw data, means, standard deviations and multiple graphs, relating analysis to the research question.
Develops a coherent argument comparing thickness and pole orientation effects with logical structure.
Identifies sources of error (timing, alignment) and suggests general improvements.
weaknesses: Analysis is largely descriptive and does not probe data scatter quantitatively.
Argumentation contains minor contradictions and lacks depth in reasoning.
Critical evaluation is superficial, with limited quantitative assessment of uncertainties.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The investigation shows clear research analysis and a structured argument (band 4â6) but does not consistently sustain depth of critical evaluation. A score of 5 reflects above-average but not fully sophisticated critical thinking across all strands.

strengths: The report follows general Extended Essay conventions and includes page numbering.
Sections are present and basic structure communicates information at a functional level.
weaknesses: Formatting is inconsistent: duplicated headings, mis-numbered tables and missing graph titles.
Graphs and tables often lack proper titles, axis labels and clear captions.
Layout issues (overlapping table cells, poor image resolution) hinder readability.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation meets the minimum standards (band 1â2) but suffers from multiple inconsistencies and missing elements that prevent clear and faultless formatting. A score of 2 out of 4 accurately captures this level of performance.

strandSelections.reasoning: Key physics terms are used but often misspelled or misapplied (âFardayâ, âelectromagnetic induction forceâ), and symbols/units are inconsistent.  Accuracy and consistency are limited, fitting the 1-2 descriptor.

There is a coherent argument (time vs thickness; pole comparison) supported by graphs, but reasoning is simplistic and sometimes contradictory.  Logical flow exists yet contains gaps; band 4-6 is appropriate, score at lower edge.

Method includes variables table, detailed materials list and step-by-step procedures; choice of timing, angle control and repeated trials shows some informed selection.  Nevertheless, timing by hand, stacking magnets as proxy for thickness, and large uncontrolled uncertainties limit appropriateness.  Mostly complete but not rigorous â mid-band.

The background section references Faradayâs law, eddy currents and Newtonâs laws and links them to the experiment, indicating reasonable subject knowledge.  Yet equations contain errors, explanations are superficial and some concepts (e.g. âcurrent in a wireâ derivation) are confused. Competent but far from excellent.

Evaluation identifies human timing error, angle misalignment and suggests improvements, but reflection is descriptive and lacks depth or quantitative consideration of uncertainty.  Fits 1-3 band, mid-score.

The student clearly identifies the general topic (magnetic braking on a copper plate) and communicates why it matters (rail-safety context). However, phrasing is grammatically weak (âHow does the duration that a person takes to slideâ¦â) and several passages are repetitive or vague.  Overall purpose is understandable but not âaccurately and effectivelyâ delivered; hence low mid-band.

Graphs and tables are present but many lack proper titles/axis labels and image resolution is poor.  Layout communicates basic information yet has notable inconsistencies, giving the lower mark in 1-2 band.

A research question is stated and is mostly focused (magnet thickness vs sliding time), yet wording is imprecise and includes an irrelevant reference to a âperson.â  Connection to subsequent discussion is present but weakened by flaws in wording and scope (pole orientation added later).  Adequate but not strong.

Raw data, means, standard deviations and multiple graphs are presented; student attempts t-test discussion.  Analysis relates to RQ but large scatter is neither deeply probed nor quantitatively modeled, so relevance is partial.  Satisfies lower end of 4-6 band.

Sections follow EE conventions and pages are numbered, but formatting is inconsistent (duplicated headings, mis-numbered tables, missing graph titles).  Adequate rather than clear and faultless â upper band 1-2.

annotations.comment: The research question phrasing is grammatically weak and overly wordy. Consider rewording to a concise question such as âHow does magnet thickness affect sliding time on a copper plate?â to improve clarity and focus.
Including the word count early fulfills formatting requirements effectively. This clear placement ensures adherence to the word-limit criterion.
The table of contents lacks page numbers and consistent indentation. Ensure each entry is accompanied by accurate page references and formatted uniformly.
The introductory anecdote about the Qalyub train disaster is engaging and demonstrates relevance to rail safety, effectively motivating the experimental focus.
Inline math for magnet thickness is truncated and incorrectly formatted. Use consistent scientific notation with units, for example $$3\times10^{-3}\,\mathrm{m}\pm1\times10^{-4}\,\mathrm{m}$$.
The section on electromagnetic induction drifts into particle physics. Streamline the background to focus on induction principles directly relevant to eddy currents.
Figure lacks a descriptive caption or axis labels. Add a clear title and label both axes to make the diagram self-explanatory.
The background on Newtonâs laws is generic. Explicitly link inertia and acceleration to the experimentâs motion of magnets to strengthen coherence.
The categorization of magnet types is correctly stated and demonstrates good foundational understanding of magnetic materials.
Section heading â2.4 Magnetic braking an eddy currentâ uses inconsistent capitalization and grammar. Standardize to âMagnetic Braking and Eddy Currentsâ.
Spelling error âFardayâs lawâ should be âFaradayâs law.â Check terminology spelling to maintain accuracy.
Equation for Faradayâs law uses \(\theta\) instead of magnetic flux \(\phi\). It should read $$\mathrm{EMF}=-N\frac{d\phi}{dt}$$ for correctness.
The variables in Faradayâs law are inconsistently denoted as \(d\theta/dT\) and \(d\phi/dT\). Standardize symbol usage throughout.
The formula $$F=BIL\sin\theta$$ is correctly stated, illustrating effective use of subject knowledge for electromagnetic forces.
Equation cites variable X in $$B=f/(I X L)$$ which is unclear. Clarify variable definitions and adopt standard notation.
Remove the trailing period in the section header â3. Experiment.â for consistency with other headings.
The aim statement is unclear about âmultiple poles.â Rephrase to specify you will investigate thickness and pole orientation effects on sliding time.
The null hypothesis incorrectly refers to constant velocity rather than no effect of thickness on time. Revise null hypothesis to state âno effect of thickness on sliding time.â
This combined description of both experiments is dense and confusing. Separate the methods for thickness and pole orientation into distinct sections.
Typographical error in dependent variable description: âdepended variableâ should read âdependent variable.â
The controlled variables paragraph is lengthy. Present each control as a bullet or table to improve readability and emphasize procedural rigor.
Materials table has overlapping cell coordinates and unclear headings. Reconstruct table so each item, measurement, and uncertainty occupies a distinct, labeled cell.
Table header rows are missing explicit column titles for materials list and uncertainties. Add descriptive headers for clarity.
Typo in step 1: âstrightâ should be âstraight.â Correcting spelling enhances presentation quality.
Procedure description B is verbose and imprecise. Break down into numbered substeps with clear actions and expected outcomes for each thickness and pole orientation.

similarExemplars.title:
How does varying the initial angular displacement of a magnetic pendulum affect
its stopping time due to damping caused by the eddy currents induced in the copper
plates below it when the string used for the pendulum, the magnet used, the
dimensions of the copper plate, and the magnetic field strength of the magnet are
kept controlled?

userSubmission.researchQuestion:
How does varying the initial angular displacement of a magnetic pendulum affect
its stopping time due to damping caused by the eddy currents induced in the copper
plates below it when the string used for the pendulum, the magnet used, the
dimensions of the copper plate, and the magnetic field strength of the magnet are
kept controlled?
